mappings.rationale: Darwin's theory of evolution transformed biology, directly relevant to the Aristotelian concepts of classification, heredity, and descent in A.1.b.
Evolution is the unifying theory of biology, directly relevant to the historical development of biological ideas about classification and heredity.
Covers the history of life on Earth, central to understanding origins and development of biological ideas.
Aristotle's concepts of classification, reproduction, heredity, and descent are specifically cited in A.1.b as foundational to biology.
Section A.1.b covers biology in the Greco-Roman world including theories about mankind and the origin of life.
Section A.1.d covers biology in the Renaissance including the scientific study of anatomy through dissection (Harvey, Vesalius, Malpighi).
Cell biology is foundational, covering the cell theory that transformed biological understanding from the 19th century onward.
The study of genes and heredity is central to the historical development of biological ideas about reproduction and descent (A.1.b).
Heredity is explicitly mentioned in A.1.b as part of Aristotelian biological concepts and is a major thread in biology's history.
Genomics represents a modern development building on the historical trajectory of genetics and molecular biology.
Molecular biology is a major branch of modern biological science emerging from the historical trajectory of biology.
Developmental biology addresses reproduction and embryology central to biology from Aristotelian times through modern science.
Systems biology represents a modern integrative approach to the biological sciences.
Synthetic biology is a contemporary frontier extending the historical trajectory of the biological sciences.
Stem cell research represents cutting-edge biological science building on cell biology and developmental biology.
Zoology as a separate discipline is mentioned in A.1.c, and animal classification is part of the Aristotelian tradition (A.1.b).
Botanical investigations are mentioned in A.1.b (Greco-Roman) and A.1.d (Renaissance dissemination of botanical knowledge).
Fungi are a major kingdom of life whose study is part of the development of botany and biological classification.
Bacteriology is a major branch of biological science that emerged with microscopy and microbiology.
Virology is a major branch of biological science, connected to microbiology and cell biology.
Microbiology is a fundamental biological science whose history includes microscopy and germ theory.
Microbiome research is a modern branch of biological science studying microbial communities.
Ecology is a major branch of the biological sciences studying organisms, communities, and ecosystems.
Entomology is a branch of zoology, which developed as a separate biological discipline (A.1.c).
Mammalogy is part of zoology, which developed alongside botany as a biological discipline (A.1.c).
The study of reptiles is part of zoology, which developed as a separate discipline (A.1.c).
The study of amphibians is part of zoology, which developed as a separate discipline (A.1.c).
Paleontological study of dinosaurs represents a major chapter in the biological sciences and history of life.
Marine biology is a major branch of the biological sciences studying life in ocean environments.
Ethology is a major branch of biological science extending from Aristotelian biological observations.
Sexual selection is a key mechanism of evolution, connected to the Darwinian tradition in biological sciences.
Human evolution is a major topic connecting classification, heredity, and descent (A.1.b).
Neuroscience is a major branch of biological science, connected to anatomical discoveries in A.1.b-d.
The study of the eye connects to anatomy and comparative biology, branches with deep historical roots.
Immunology is a major branch of biological science studying defense mechanisms at the cellular and molecular level.
Endocrinology is a branch of biological science studying hormonal regulation, connected to physiology.
Enzyme biochemistry is fundamental, connecting chemistry and biology at the molecular level.
Hematology is part of the biological sciences, connected to the study of physiology and anatomy.
Circadian biology studies internal biological clocks and their molecular regulation.
The study of teeth connects to comparative anatomy, evolution, and paleontology within biological sciences.
Olfactory biology involves neuroscience, physiology, and sensory biology, all branches of the biological sciences.
Forest ecology studies plant communities and ecosystem dynamics as a major branch of biological science.
Coral reef biology is part of marine biology and ecology, major branches of the biological sciences.
Biodiversity conservation applies biological science principles to the preservation of species diversity.
The study of invasive species is a branch of ecology and conservation biology.
Extinction studies combine paleontology, ecology, and evolutionary biology.
Biogeography studies the distribution of biological species, connecting ecology, evolution, and geography.
Symbiosis is a key concept in ecology and evolutionary biology.
Microscopy has been essential to biological sciences, enabling study of cells, microorganisms, and anatomical structures.
Addresses philosophical questions about classification, evolution, and the nature of life arising from biological sciences.
Astrobiology extends the biological sciences to questions about the origin and possibility of life beyond Earth.
Eugenics represents a historical application of biological ideas about heredity and descent.
Pavlov's experimental work in physiology contributed to the development of biological sciences and animal behavior study.
Mathematical biology applies mathematical models to biological problems including epidemiology, ecology, and pattern formation.
